proposals.method: We will extend the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) framework by integrating a robust loss function, such as Generalized Cross Entropy, which is less sensitive to label noise. Concurrently, we will adapt influence function techniques—building on Perez's prior work—to identify and dynamically down-weight or filter training examples whose gradients indicate high susceptibility to noise or adversarial influence. We will pretrain medium-scale language models (e.g., 1-7B parameters) on synthetically corrupted versions of public preference datasets (e.g., Anthropic-HH) and evaluate robustness on held-out tasks measuring reasoning faithfulness and safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, integrated method for robust preference-aligned pretraining, 2) empirical benchmarks quantifying model degradation under various data corruption types, and 3) open-source tools for diagnosing noise in preference datasets. It directly addresses a practical bottleneck in creating reliable, aligned AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While robust losses and influence functions have been studied separately, their combined application to the specific problem of noisy human preference data during the pretraining stage—a critical phase for instilling values—is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc fine-tuning corrections to build robustness into the foundational alignment process.

proposals.title: Generalization Bottlenecks in Reward-Model-Guided Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as 'Pretraining Language Models with Human Preferences', demonstrates that integrating reward model (RM) feedback directly into pretraining can improve alignment. However, the generalization properties of the RM itself are poorly understood. If the RM fails to generalize beyond its training distribution, it could introduce systematic biases or misalignment into the foundation model during pretraining, compromising its safety and utility on novel tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generalization capacity of the reward model—specifically its performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) preference data—is a primary bottleneck determining the final pretrained model's alignment and robustness on held-out tasks.
proposals.method: We will systematically vary the scope (e.g., diversity of annotators, task domains) and quality (e.g., label noise, consistency) of the data used to train a suite of reward models. Using these RMs, we will conduct scaled-down pretraining runs of transformer-based language models, following the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) or reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) paradigm integrated into the pretraining loop. We will then evaluate the resulting foundation models on a battery of OOD tasks, measuring both performance and alignment metrics (e.g., truthfulness, harmlessness), and analyze the correlation with RM generalization error using influence functions to trace model decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for auditing reward model generalization as a safety-critical component of the pretraining pipeline. It will yield concrete guidelines for constructing RMs that yield more robustly aligned foundation models and establish diagnostic tools to predict alignment failures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies RM generalization in fine-tuning, its critical role during the foundational pretraining stage is unexplored. Our approach uniquely connects RM data properties to final model alignment through controlled ablation and causal tracing techniques.

proposals.title: Static vs. Dynamic Alignment: Benchmarking Conditional Pretraining Against Online Preference Collection
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are aligned with human preferences via static reward models (RMs) trained on fixed datasets, a process that may not capture evolving or nuanced human judgments. As pretraining scales, this static approach risks embedding suboptimal or misgeneralized preferences. A direct comparison between this dominant paradigm and methods that integrate human feedback dynamically during pretraining is critically needed to understand the trade-offs in alignment quality and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that online methods for collecting and integrating human feedback during pretraining will yield models with superior generalization on held-out preference tasks and greater robustness to distribution shift compared to models conditioned on a static, pretrained reward model.
proposals.method: We will implement two agent architectures based on a common transformer backbone (e.g., GPT-NeoX). The 'Static' model will be pretrained with conditional modeling on a fixed dataset of human preferences (e.g., Anthropic Helpful & Harmless). The 'Dynamic' model will undergo standard pretraining interleaved with simulated online feedback rounds using a synthetic human-in-the-loop (e.g., a preference model updated via RLHF on a stream of new queries). We will benchmark both on a suite of tasks measuring preference adherence, reasoning faithfulness (inspired by Perez's prior work), and out-of-distribution generalization, using influence functions to analyze training dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, empirical comparison of static and dynamic human preference integration during LLM pretraining. It will yield concrete guidance on the sample efficiency and final performance trade-offs of each paradigm. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation alignment protocols for foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied online RLHF during fine-tuning, systematically benchmarking online feedback collection *during* the core pretraining phase—and contrasting it with conditional pretraining—is an unexplored and non-trivial comparison central to scalable alignment.

proposals.title: Inferring Implicit Human Preferences: Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) pretraining uses a simple next-token prediction objective, which does not explicitly capture the complex, multi-dimensional human preferences embedded in high-quality text corpora. Explicitly learning a reward function from these demonstrations could lead to models with better alignment and reasoning capabilities from the outset, reducing the need for extensive post-hoc alignment. This addresses a core challenge in creating more capable and reliable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to the pretraining corpus will allow us to infer a latent reward function representing human preferences, and integrating this reward signal into the pretraining objective will produce language models that demonstrate improved generalization, reasoning faithfulness, and alignment on downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Maximum Entropy IRL framework to operate on sequences of text tokens. Using a frozen, pretrained base model as the 'expert demonstrator' for the high-quality pretraining corpus, we will train a separate reward model to infer the underlying preferences. This reward model will be integrated into the pretraining loop via a modified objective combining next-token prediction loss with a reward-weighted term, encouraging the model to generate sequences with high inferred reward. We will experiment with scaling this approach using distributed training on large-scale datasets like The Pile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for integrating human preference signals directly into the pretraining stage, potentially improving the sample efficiency and final alignment of LLMs. It will yield new insights into the nature of the preferences encoded in web-scale text data and establish a benchmark for evaluating the impact of IRL-based pretraining on downstream reasoning and generalization tasks.
proposals.novelty: While IRL has been explored in robotics and for fine-tuning LLMs via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), its direct application to infer a reward function from the raw pretraining corpus itself is novel. This moves beyond using human feedback as a separate post-pretraining signal and instead treats the entire pretraining dataset as a set of implicit preference demonstrations.

proposals.title: Active Preference Sampling: Efficiently Integrating Human Feedback into Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Integrating human preferences directly into pretraining, as explored in Perez et al.'s 'Pretraining Language Models with Human Preferences,' is a promising path toward aligning models with human values from the ground up. However, collecting high-quality preference feedback at the scale of pretraining is prohibitively expensive. Current methods typically sample data uniformly, which is inefficient for learning nuanced preferences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using active learning to strategically select the most informative or uncertain text segments for human preference feedback will significantly improve the sample efficiency and final alignment of pretrained language models compared to uniform sampling.
proposals.method: We will build upon a standard autoregressive pretraining pipeline (e.g., GPT-3 architecture). During training, we will interleave active learning cycles: a candidate pool of text sequences will be scored by the in-training model using an acquisition function, such as predictive entropy or a committee-based disagreement measure from an ensemble of model checkpoints. The top-k most uncertain sequences will be presented for human preference judgments (e.g., pairwise comparisons). These new preference labels will then be integrated into the pretraining objective via a reward modeling and KL-constrained reinforcement learning loss, as in prior preference-based pretraining work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for making human-in-the-loop pretraining practically feasible by reducing feedback costs. It will yield new insights into which data points are most valuable for learning human preferences and demonstrate measurable gains in model alignment and generalization on held-out preference benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-studied for supervised fine-tuning, its application to preference-based pretraining—where the feedback signal is comparative and the model is learning from scratch—is novel. This combines Perez's expertise in influence functions (for understanding generalization) and preference-based pretraining in a new, efficiency-focused direction.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Preference-Conditioned Pretraining: Emergent Alignment in Trillion-Parameter Language Models
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for next-token prediction are well-established, but the scaling behavior of models pretrained with explicit human preference signals remains unexplored. As model sizes approach the trillion-parameter scale, understanding whether and how alignment properties emerge directly from scaled conditional pretraining, rather than post-hoc fine-tuning, is a critical open question for developing capable and reliable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for models of sufficient scale (approaching 1T parameters), conditional pretraining on a mixture of next-token prediction and human preference prediction will induce emergent alignment properties, leading to superior performance and sample efficiency on downstream human-preferred tasks compared to standard scaling followed by reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).
proposals.method: We will extend the conditional pretraining method from 'Pretraining Language Models with Human Preferences' to models scaled from 10B to ~1T parameters using dense transformer architectures. We will train on a massive corpus where a subset of data is paired with synthetic or human preference scores, using a multi-task objective combining language modeling and preference prediction. We will empirically derive scaling laws for both loss and downstream alignment metrics, and analyze emergent capabilities using the influence function techniques from Perez's prior work to trace the effect of preference data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first scaling laws for preference-conditioned pretraining, providing a quantitative framework for future aligned model development. It will offer empirical evidence on whether 'alignment by construction' via scaled conditional pretraining is a viable alternative to the dominant RLHF paradigm, potentially reducing the complexity of the alignment pipeline.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for autoregressive pretraining are known, applying this analysis to conditional pretraining with human preferences is novel. Furthermore, investigating alignment as an emergent property of scaled conditional training, rather than a separate fine-tuning stage, challenges the standard pre-train-then-align methodology dominant pre-2026.

proposals.title: Stress-Testing Safety via Adversarial Pretraining: Generating a Corpus of Maximally Undesirable Text
proposals.motivation: Current safety alignment techniques, such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), are evaluated on existing datasets, which may not expose worst-case failures. A systematic method to generate challenging, model-written undesirable content is needed to robustly stress-test safety classifiers and reward models, identifying failure modes before deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, pretrained with an objective to maximize the generation of text classified as undesirable by a set of pre-2026 safety filters, will produce a uniquely challenging adversarial corpus that reveals systematic weaknesses in current safety evaluation paradigms.
proposals.method: We will adapt the pretraining objective from Perez et al.'s 'Pretraining Language Models with Human Preferences' but invert the reward signal. Starting from a base model (e.g., GPT-2 architecture), we will train on a standard corpus (e.g., C4) using a reward-weighted loss, where the reward is provided by an ensemble of frozen safety classifiers (e.g., Perspective API, Detoxify) and reward models from published RLHF work. The training objective will explicitly maximize the probability of tokens that receive high 'undesirability' scores, iteratively refining the model's generations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, model-generated 'adversarial undesirable' dataset for stress-testing, 2) a quantitative analysis of failure modes in current safety filters when faced with adaptive generations, and 3) a methodological framework for proactive safety evaluation inspired by adversarial machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies generalization or measures faithfulness, this proposal actively engineers a model to be adversarially undesirable from pretraining, creating a dynamic benchmark rather than using static datasets. It frames safety evaluation as a dual to standard alignment, inverting the preference optimization objective.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Preference Pairs: Using Large Language Models to Bootstrap Reward Model Training
proposals.motivation: Training reward models for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is bottlenecked by the cost and scale of high-quality human preference annotations. This limits the diversity and complexity of tasks for which alignment can be effectively learned. Automating the generation of synthetic preference data could dramatically scale and improve preference learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A large language model (LLM), guided by a small seed of human preferences and principles of contrastive generation, can synthesize high-quality, diverse preference pairs that are effective for training a robust reward model, reducing reliance on extensive human annotation.
proposals.method: We will use a strong pretrained LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Claude) as a synthetic data generator. Starting from a small seed dataset of human-annotated (chosen, rejected) response pairs, we will prompt the LLM to generate new queries and corresponding response pairs that exhibit clear qualitative differences (e.g., helpfulness, harmlessness, correctness). We will employ techniques like chain-of-thought prompting to articulate the preference rationale and use iterative filtering based on a preliminary reward model to ensure quality. The synthesized dataset will then be used to train a separate reward model, whose performance will be evaluated on held-out human annotations and downstream RLHF policy training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method for cheaply scaling preference data for RLHF, a systematic evaluation of synthetic preference data quality, and insights into the bootstrapping capabilities of LLMs for alignment tasks. It directly addresses a key scalability challenge in training aligned AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used to generate training data for standard tasks, their application to generate the nuanced, comparative preference pairs required for reward modeling is largely unexplored. This inverts the typical RLHF pipeline, using the model to be aligned to create its own training signal.

proposals.title: Measuring Bias Amplification in LM-Generated Evaluation Datasets
proposals.motivation: The use of large language models (LLMs) to generate evaluation datasets and scoring rubrics is a rapidly growing practice to scale up AI assessment. However, if these LM-written evals simply codify the social, cultural, and factual biases present in the generator model's own training data, they risk creating a closed, self-reinforcing loop that systematically disadvantages certain outputs. This could undermine the reliability of benchmark progress and lead to models that perform well only on evaluations that reflect a narrow, potentially biased worldview.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLM-generated evaluation criteria and scoring will systematically amplify the biases present in the generator model's pretraining data, leading to measurable performance disparities for model outputs aligned with underrepresented or dispreferred viewpoints.
proposals.method: We will use a suite of pre-2026 bias probes (e.g., for gender, race, and political slant) to characterize biases in a generator LLM like GPT-4. This generator will then produce evaluation rubrics and score responses for a diverse set of reasoning and open-ended generation tasks. We will train multiple smaller models (e.g., LLaMA variants) using Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), but replace human preferences with scores from the biased generator. Finally, we will apply influence function analysis and out-of-distribution testing to measure how the generator's biases propagate into the trained models' outputs and generalization patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for quantifying bias propagation in LM-as-evaluator pipelines. It will yield concrete metrics for 'evaluation bias amplification' and demonstrate the risks of uncritically using LLMs for automated evaluation. The findings will inform safer protocols for creating synthetic evaluation data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies bias in models or uses LMs for evaluation, no pre-2026 research systematically tests how an LM evaluator's biases are *transferred and amplified* during the training of a new model, creating a novel measurement paradigm for closed-loop AI development risks.

proposals.title: Dynamic Adversarial Evaluation for Online Learning of Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations for large language models (LLMs) are largely static, failing to adapt to a model's specific strengths and weaknesses as they are fine-tuned online with human feedback. This creates a mismatch between training and assessment, potentially allowing models to exploit narrow task formulations without developing robust, generalizable reasoning. Grounded in Perez's prior work on faithfulness in reasoning and human preference pretraining, this project addresses the need for evaluations that actively probe and challenge a model's evolving capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an evaluation framework which dynamically generates adversarial follow-up questions based on a model's previous responses will more accurately and efficiently measure true generalization and robustness than static benchmarks, revealing failure modes that standard evaluations miss.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-agent system: a 'Prober' LLM and a 'Subject' LLM (the model under evaluation). The Prober will be trained using reinforcement learning, with a reward function based on its success in eliciting incorrect or inconsistent responses from the Subject. Starting from a seed question, the Prober will generate adaptive follow-ups that target ambiguities, contradictions, or over-generalizations in the Subject's answers. We will ground this in influence function analysis to trace how specific training examples affect vulnerability to certain adversarial probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel paradigm for adaptive evaluation, providing a tool for more rigorous safety and capability auditing during online learning. It will yield new insights into the relationship between training data, learned representations, and failure modes, advancing beyond static benchmark performance as the primary metric for LLM progress.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks exist, this approach uniquely integrates them into a continuous, online evaluation loop that co-evolves with model training, moving beyond one-off static test sets. It combines Perez's expertise in reasoning faithfulness and influence functions to create a diagnostic, not just a stress test.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Evaluators: Can Small Language Models Generate Faithful and High-Quality Task Evaluations?
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of large language models (LLMs) has made them the de facto standard for generating evaluation data and scoring model outputs, but this is computationally expensive and raises centralization concerns. This creates a significant barrier for research groups with limited resources. A pre-2026 research gap is whether smaller, more efficient models can produce evaluations of comparable quality, democratizing access to high-quality feedback loops for model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small LMs (e.g., <3B parameters), when specifically fine-tuned on high-quality human or large-model feedback, can generate evaluation scores and critiques that are as faithful and predictive of human judgment as those from much larger, general-purpose LLMs.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a suite of small LMs (e.g., Pythia, GPT-2 variants) on datasets of human preferences and chain-of-thought reasoning critiques, such as those from Anthropic's HH-RLHF or OpenAI's Summarization datasets. Using influence functions, we will analyze which training examples most affect the small LM's evaluation performance. The core experiment will benchmark these small evaluators against GPT-3.5/4 and human judges on held-out reasoning and dialogue tasks, measuring correlation (e.g., Spearman's ρ) and the faithfulness of their generated rationales.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical framework for assessing small-model evaluators, potentially identifying a Pareto frontier of evaluation quality vs. model size. It will contribute a publicly released suite of efficient, fine-tuned evaluator models and a diagnostic of the data characteristics necessary for training them.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs as evaluators, the systematic investigation of downscaling this capability and analyzing the training dynamics (via influence functions) to create efficient, specialized evaluators is novel. It directly tests the assumption that scale is necessary for reliable evaluation.

proposals.title: Auditing Human-Written Benchmarks: A Language Model-Based Framework for Detecting Ambiguity, Bias, and Reasoning Shortcuts
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of language models is benchmarked against static, human-written evaluation datasets. However, pre-2026 research has shown these datasets can contain subtle biases, ambiguities, and unintended 'shortcut' patterns that models can exploit, leading to inflated performance metrics that do not reflect true reasoning or generalization. Systematically auditing these datasets at scale is a major unsolved challenge for the field.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), fine-tuned with human feedback on dataset quality, can be used as scalable, automated auditors to identify and characterize systematic flaws—such as spurious correlations, ambiguous instructions, and demographic biases—within established human-written evaluation benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will adapt and fine-tune a pretrained LLM (e.g., a T5 or GPT variant) using a novel training task derived from human preference data on dataset quality. The model will be trained to generate structured critiques (e.g., identifying shortcut cues, rating ambiguity, flagging potential bias) for individual test items from datasets like MMLU, HellaSwag, and TruthfulQA. We will validate the model's critiques against human judgments and use influence function analysis to trace how specific flawed items disproportionately affect model performance estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable tool for the community to audit and improve evaluation benchmarks, leading to more reliable measurement of LM capabilities. It will also yield a novel diagnostic of how dataset construction artifacts influence perceived model performance, advancing the science of evaluation itself.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs are commonly the *subjects* of evaluation, using them as *critics* of the evaluation instruments themselves reverses the standard paradigm. This approach leverages the models' own pattern recognition and reasoning abilities to expose the limitations of the human-created tests designed to assess them.

proposals.title: Closing the Loop: Training Language Models on Self-Discovered Failure Modes from LM-Written Evaluations
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate and critique their own outputs, creating automated evaluation pipelines. However, these discovered failure modes are typically used only for diagnostic purposes, not for direct model improvement. This represents a missed opportunity to create a self-correcting training loop, especially as scaling human feedback becomes a bottleneck for aligning models on complex, open-ended tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: That failure cases systematically identified by an LM-as-a-judge can be converted into effective training data, and that fine-tuning on this synthesized data will directly and measurably reduce the prevalence of those specific undesirable behaviors without degrading general performance.
proposals.method: We will first generate a diverse set of model outputs for a target task (e.g., chain-of-thought reasoning). A separate, prompted LLM judge will then critique these outputs to identify and categorize specific failure modes (e.g., unfaithful reasoning steps). For each failure, we will use the critique to synthesize a corrected example, creating a contrastive (failure, correction) pair. We will then fine-tune the original model on these pairs using a standard supervised loss, possibly augmented with a preference modeling objective like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to strengthen the contrast. Performance will be assessed on held-out evaluation sets and via influence function analysis to trace the impact of the synthetic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology for turning diagnostic evaluation into a corrective training signal, advancing beyond static benchmarking. It will yield insights into whether models can effectively learn from their own self-critique and establish a framework for more iterative, automated alignment pipelines that reduce reliance on exhaustive human annotation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for evaluation or generates data for pretraining, the key novelty is the targeted, closed-loop use of *failure-mode critiques* as the exclusive source for corrective fine-tuning, directly addressing the inverse problem of turning a diagnostic into a training objective.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Preference Optimization for Faithful and Abstractive Long-Form Generation
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task learning for long-form QA often uses simple loss summation, which can lead to trade-offs where improved abstractiveness comes at the cost of factual faithfulness. Pre-2026 work, including Perez's on faithfulness in CoT and pretraining with human preferences, highlights the critical need for generation that is both creative and reliable. This gap limits the trustworthy deployment of LLMs in domains requiring detailed, accurate explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the multi-objective nature of long-form generation—balancing abstractiveness, faithfulness, and coherence—through novel training objectives derived from human preference data will yield superior performance compared to standard multi-task learning.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of auxiliary objectives based on pre-2026 methods. This includes: 1) A contrastive faithfulness objective using influence functions to upweight training examples that promote factually consistent generations. 2) A distributional matching objective, using KL-divergence, to align the model's abstractive style with human reference distributions without mere copying. 3) A preference-aware gradient blending mechanism, inspired by multi-task optimization literature, to dynamically balance these objectives during training. We will fine-tune a large language model (e.g., T5 or GPT-style) on datasets like ELI5 or ASQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for multi-objective fine-tuning that explicitly negotiates key trade-offs in long-form generation. It will deliver a model with quantitatively improved faithfulness and abstractiveness, alongside a methodological advance in integrating human preference signals into multi-task learning setups.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common, formulating abstractive generation as a preference-driven multi-objective optimization problem, using influence functions for faithfulness guidance and dynamic gradient balancing, is a novel synthesis of pre-2026 techniques.

proposals.title: Generating Faithful Long-Form Explanations in Technical Domains via Preference-Tuned Specialized Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often produce plausible but unfaithful or generic explanations in complex domains like law and medicine, limiting their reliability for expert use. While instruction tuning improves general task performance, it does not guarantee the factual grounding and technical precision required in these high-stakes fields. This work addresses the critical need for models that can generate detailed, accurate, and domain-faithful long-form answers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model, first pretrained on general text and then fine-tuned with human preference feedback on a focused dataset of expert-curated technical explanations, will generate more faithful and higher-quality long-form answers in that domain than a model tuned only for general instruction following.
proposals.method: We will create a high-quality dataset of long-form Q&A pairs in a specific technical domain (e.g., U.S. contract law), curated and validated by domain experts. We will then adapt the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) algorithm to train a specialized LLM (e.g., based on Llama 2 or a similar pre-2026 base model). The training will use expert rankings of explanation quality, faithfulness, and technical depth as preference pairs. We will evaluate using both automated metrics (e.g., FactScore, citation accuracy) and expert judgments, comparing against strong instruction-tuned baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, expert-validated dataset for technical long-form generation, 2) a methodology for applying preference optimization to enforce domain-specific faithfulness, and 3) an analysis of how specialization affects generalization within and outside the target domain, extending influence function-based generalization studies to technical reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining human preference tuning—typically used for alignment and safety—with the explicit goal of enhancing factual faithfulness and technical depth in a narrow domain, moving beyond general instruction following to create a model specialized for expert-level explanatory tasks.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Faithfulness: Scaling Long-Form Question Answering with Multi-Lingual Retrieval and Preference-Tuned Generation
proposals.motivation: Long-form QA (LFQA) benchmarks like ELI5 evaluate in-depth reasoning and generation in English, but their extension to multiple languages remains limited. This gap prevents a systematic evaluation of whether large language models (LLMs) can produce faithful, high-quality answers across diverse linguistic and cultural contexts, which is critical for global applications. Scaling LFQA multilingually tests core capabilities in cross-lingual retrieval and generation that are underexplored in pre-2026 work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling LFQA to multiple languages will reveal significant performance disparities driven by language resource availability, and that training LLMs with cross-lingual retrieval-augmented generation and human preference feedback can significantly improve answer faithfulness and quality in lower-resource languages.
proposals.method: We will create a multi-lingual LFQA dataset by translating and culturally adapting a subset of ELI5 questions and gold answers into a typologically diverse set of languages (e.g., Spanish, Swahili, Hindi). We will implement a two-stage model: first, a dense retriever (based on Contriever or mContriever) fetches relevant English and target-language documents. Second, a multilingual LLM (e.g., BLOOM or XGLM), fine-tuned for generation, will be trained to produce answers conditioned on these documents. Inspired by Perez's prior work, we will apply preference optimization techniques (e.g., Direct Preference Optimization) using human or model-generated feedback to improve answer faithfulness and coherence across languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly available benchmark for multi-lingual long-form QA requiring cross-lingual retrieval and generation; 2) an analysis of how LLM generalization and faithfulness, as studied via influence functions, varies across languages; and 3) a demonstrated method for improving cross-lingual QA using retrieval-augmentation and human preferences.
proposals.novelty: While multi-lingual QA exists for short answers, scaling the complex, document-grounded long-form QA task is novel. The integration of cross-lingual retrieval with preference-based tuning specifically for improving faithfulness in multi-lingual generation is a non-obvious synthesis of Perez's research themes.

ideas.title: Task-Agnostic Learning via Meta-Architecture Search
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that few-shot learning requires selecting from a set of human-written, task-specific prompts. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and protein engineering—where directed evolution and computational design discover functional protein sequences for novel substrates without full pre-specification—we adapt this solution to machine learning. The adapted research idea is to develop a 'Task-Agnostic Learning via Meta-Architecture Search.' Instead of using a fixed prompt library, the model incorporates a meta-architecture that, when presented with a few examples of a novel task, conducts an internal, efficient search over possible prompt representations or computational pathways. This search is driven by a general, task-agnostic meta-loss (e.g., maximizing the mutual information between the support examples and the model's adaptation, or minimizing description length of the solution). The outcome is a method that generates a context-adapted 'prompt' or inference procedure dynamically for each new task, moving beyond selection from a human-curated set and towards true, flexible few-shot learning.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning a Model-Generating Hypernetwork for Few-Shot Adaptation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to meta-learn a hypernetwork that, given a few examples from a new case, directly generates the parameters of a single, task-adapted model. It inverts the traditional selection or fine-tuning process by learning a mapping from support sets to complete model parameters. The hypernetwork is trained across many tasks to produce models that are not just performant but also inherently reliable and robust to the ambiguities of few-shot data. This results in a system that performs fast, one-step adaptation with a guaranteed, well-formed output model, effectively solving the model selection and adaptation problems simultaneously.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Self-Improving Case Memory in KBQA
ideas.description: This research direction integrates meta-learning with KBQA to create a system that learns a dual-purpose adaptation policy. From few successful QA sessions, the meta-learner infers both an optimal prompting strategy for immediate answers and a set of rules for repairing or expanding the underlying case memory (e.g., by identifying missing entities, correcting faulty relations, or adding new semantic clusters). This turns every user interaction into a potential training signal for both task performance and long-term knowledge base integrity. Consequently, the system achieves robust few-shot adaptation while its core memory autonomously evolves to become more comprehensive and accurate, reducing future errors.

ideas.title: Constraint-Completion Retrieval via Latent Structural Gap Prediction
ideas.description: This system retrieves documents based on their ability to fill identifiable structural gaps in a query's representation, moving beyond surface similarity. It employs a dual-encoder architecture where one model analyzes the query to predict a set of missing compositional constraints, and the other evaluates candidate passages for how well they satisfy those constraints. The retriever is trained end-to-end using reinforcement learning, where the reward is derived from the performance improvement of a downstream reasoner that uses the retrieved evidence. This creates a concrete, task-driven objective for 'complementarity,' ensuring retrievals are both structurally relevant and pragmatically useful.

ideas.title: Hyperparameter-Agnostic Prompting via Self-Calibrating Ensembles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hyperparameters (e.g., shot count, example order, format) must be carefully selected for few-shot learning. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering's use of passive tuned mass dampers—which automatically stabilize structures against variable loads without manual adjustment—we propose designing self-calibrating prompt ensembles. The solution involves crafting a meta-prompt that embeds a diverse set of few-shot strategies (varying shots, perspectives, formats) as internal components. The LM is then instructed to use its own reasoning to dynamically emphasize or combine these strategies based on the presented query, effectively making the process invariant to the traditional hyperparameter choices and eliminating the need for a validation set to tune them.

ideas.title: Hybrid Meta-Learning with Non-Parametric Validation for Robust Few-Shot Case Adaptation
ideas.description: This system combines meta-learning for fast initial adaptation with a robust validation step using a non-parametric memory. For a new case, a meta-learned algorithm proposes a model update from few examples. A gating network then retrieves analogous prototypes from the full case memory and computes a consistency score. The final adaptation is a weighted combination of the meta-update and a non-parametric adjustment based on the prototypes, ensuring the adaptation is both data-efficient and generalizable, avoiding overfitting to spurious patterns.

ideas.title: Explore FiLM for few-shot and zero-shot learning
ideas.description: Systematically investigate FiLM's few-shot and zero-shot generalization capabilities on diverse, real-world datasets.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian Optimization with a Surrogate Model from Drug Discovery for Few-Shot Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational drug discovery to the problem of few-shot selection. In drug discovery, Bayesian optimization with a Gaussian Process surrogate model is used to navigate the high-dimensional chemical space and identify promising drug candidates with minimal costly lab experiments. Analogously, we can frame the selection of few-shot examples or prompt hyperparameters as searching a high-dimensional 'prompt space'. A surrogate model (e.g., a GP) is trained on initial evaluations to predict a target LLM's performance based on embeddings or features of the candidate examples/prompts. An acquisition function then guides the selection of the next, most informative batch to evaluate on the actual model. This creates an efficient, closed-loop system that finds high-performing few-shot configurations with a drastically reduced number of expensive model inferences, directly transferring the batch-efficient, experiment-minimizing philosophy from drug discovery to NLP.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning with Episodic Curriculum from Immunology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational immunology, which models how the immune system performs fast adaptation to new pathogens using immunological memory. The analogous solution involves a two-phase process mirroring primary and secondary immune responses: a slow, broad training phase to build general capabilities, followed by a rapid, context-driven adaptation phase. Adapted to meta-learning for case memory, the method involves first meta-training a model on a diverse set of tasks to learn robust initial parameters (the 'primary response'). When presented with few examples from a new case, the system doesn't just apply standard few-shot gradient updates. Instead, it uses the new case examples to generate a context signal. This context selectively activates and guides the fine-tuning of a sparse, modular subset of the model's parameters—akin to activating specific memory B-cell clones—enabling more efficient, targeted, and faster adaptation than full-model fine-tuning, directly leveraging the structured 'memory' of past cases.

ideas.title: Conversational Meta-Selection for Adaptive Few-Shot Systems
ideas.description: This research develops an interactive AI system that uses a conversational interface to refine case-based reasoning through dialogue. Crucially, the dialogue agent itself employs novel, theoretically-grounded few-shot model selection criteria to dynamically choose and adapt its own prompting strategies, retrieval parameters, and refinement tactics during the conversation. This enables the system to perform online meta-optimization, learning the most effective interaction policies for the specific user and problem context without any pre-collected validation data. The result is a self-improving conversational agent that co-evolves its case refinement and internal decision-making processes simultaneously through interaction.

ideas.title: Meta-Trainable Dense Retriever for Few-Shot Case Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-learning framework for dense passage retrieval models used in case search. The core idea is to train the retriever's encoder such that, given a few annotated query-document pairs from a new case (e.g., a new legal domain or a specific client's document history), it can quickly adapt its representation space to maximize relevance for that specific context. This combines the semantic matching power of dense retrieval with the fast adaptation capability of meta-learning. The resulting system would enable precise, personalized case retrieval that evolves dynamically with minimal new data, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all semantic model.

ideas.title: Inductive Program Synthesis for Task-Agnostic Few-Shot Adaptation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a method where, given a few retrieved examples of a novel task, an inductive program synthesis engine generates a general, executable program (or logical form) that performs the task. This synthesized program acts as the reasoning scaffold, replacing the need for a manually engineered prompt. The synergy enables true few-shot learning by moving beyond pattern imitation in a fixed model to the automatic creation of a verified, task-specific reasoning procedure from minimal demonstrations.

ideas.title: Synthesize Natural Language Questions via Constrained Decoding from Logic
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from inverse molecular design in computational chemistry to the task of generating natural language questions from logic. In the analogous field, target properties act as constraints to guide the generation of valid molecular structures. Here, the logical form or KB fact provides semantic constraints (predicates, entities, relations) and syntactic constraints (quantifiers, logical operators). The adapted research idea is to train a neural generator with a specialized constrained decoding mechanism. This mechanism uses the logical form to create a mask or a guiding objective over the vocabulary during question generation, ensuring the output question is a valid linguistic realization of the input logic, solving the inverse problem through guided synthesis rather than retrieval.

ideas.title: Ensemble of Few-Shot Adapted Experts with Meta-Cognitive Gating
ideas.description: This system inverts model selection by generating a dynamic set of specialized model adaptations from the few-shot context. Instead of a single point solution, it creates a diverse ensemble where each member represents a different plausible interpretation of the task defined by the examples. A lightweight meta-gating network, trained to predict specialist reliability, then combines their outputs for each input, providing robust and calibrated predictions. This approach yields the adaptability of few-shot generation while maintaining the stability, uncertainty awareness, and fallback robustness of ensemble-based systems.

ideas.title: Few-Shot Model Generation via Immune System-Inspired Affinity Maturation
ideas.description: This research inverts the model selection problem by drawing an analogy from immunology's adaptive immune system. The analogous solution is how vertebrates generate specific antibodies: rather than selecting from a pre-existing vast set, they create initial antibodies via V(D)J recombination and then rapidly adapt them through somatic hypermutation and affinity maturation based on limited pathogen exposure. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to design a meta-architecture that generates a single task-adapted model from few-shot examples alone. This involves a two-phase process: first, a 'recombination' mechanism assembles a rough prototype model by combining modular neural components (like layers or attention heads) based on the examples. Second, a constrained 'hypermutation' phase performs rapid, targeted parameter adjustments only in specific, task-relevant regions of the prototype, mimicking affinity maturation. The outcome is a reliably specialized model generated directly from the context, eliminating the need to select from or fine-tune a large set of pre-trained models.

ideas.title: Parametric Solver with Nonparametric Judicial Review
ideas.description: This idea inverts the classic roles of parametric and nonparametric components by drawing an analogy from legal systems. In common law, a statutory code provides the primary, parametric rules for decision-making, while case law acts as a nonparametric memory for refinement and verification. The analogous solution uses precedents not to solve every case, but to interpret the code and handle exceptions. Adapted to machine learning, this means training a robust parametric model (e.g., a deep neural network) to serve as the primary, efficient solver. A nonparametric case memory is then employed in a strictly secondary, 'judicial review' role: a verifier module flags predictions where the parametric model's confidence is low or its output is anomalous, triggering a search of the case memory for similar, historically resolved instances to correct or justify the final output. This shifts the system's assumption from 'solve by analogy first' to 'solve by learned rule, refine by analogy only when necessary.'

ideas.title: Bayesian Portfolio Optimization for Few-Shot Model Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to portfolio optimization under uncertainty from financial management to the problem of model selection in the true few-shot setting. In finance, Bayesian methods like the Black-Litterman model formally incorporate the uncertainty of investor views to create robust portfolios. Similarly, we propose to develop a model selection framework that treats each candidate model's few-shot performance as an uncertain observation. By establishing a prior distribution over model performance (derived from pre-training trends or meta-knowledge) and updating it with the noisy few-shot evaluation results (likelihood), we compute a posterior distribution. The selection criterion then uses this posterior—considering both mean and variance—to choose the model that maximizes a risk-aware utility function, such as an expected improvement or a lower confidence bound, thereby more effectively accounting for performance uncertainty than methods based on point estimates alone.

ideas.title: Confidence-Aware Dynamic Arbitration Between Parametric and Case-Based Reasoning
ideas.description: This system prevents parametric model overreach by not fixing the hierarchical roles. Instead, a lightweight meta-controller analyzes the input to assess the parametric model's epistemic confidence and the density and relevance of neighboring cases in the nonparametric memory. Based on this analysis, it dynamically assigns the 'primary solver' role. This ensures robust performance on both common tasks (leveraging parametric efficiency) and edge cases (leveraging memory-based specificity), creating a self-correcting architecture that mitigates the inherent biases of a static hierarchy.

ideas.title: Web-Scale Temporal Case Memory for Evolving Knowledge QA
ideas.description: This research direction investigates using the entire web as a dynamic, temporal case memory for question answering. Instead of updating a fixed knowledge base schema, the system continuously indexes web-scale corpora with temporal metadata, treating past text as stored 'cases' of how knowledge was expressed at different times. For a query, it retrieves and synthesizes evidence from relevant time periods, enabling it to answer questions about evolving entities (e.g., 'Who was the CEO of X?') and handle schema changes (e.g., new relation types) by finding analogous descriptive patterns in the corpus. This approach bypasses the need for explicit KB construction and updates, directly leveraging the implicit, noisy, but temporally rich record of the web to model knowledge evolution.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning a Prompt Selection Policy via Adaptive Clinical Trial Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies meta-learning to learn how to select prompts or hyperparameters from few examples, drawing an analogy from adaptive clinical trial design in biostatistics. In that field, trials use pre-specified rules and Bayesian models to modify the experimental protocol (e.g., which treatment to allocate more patients to) based on interim analyses of limited early data. The adapted solution trains a meta-learner on a distribution of few-shot tasks to internalize a policy for prompt/hyperparameter selection. This policy acts like an adaptive trial designer: given the first few examples (interim data) of a new task, it performs a rapid analysis to select the most promising next prompts or adjustments, optimizing for final task accuracy in a data-efficient, principled manner.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate faithful CoT

ideas.title: Pragmatic, Goal-Driven Question Generation from Logical Forms
ideas.description: This system generates natural language questions from logical forms or KB facts by explicitly modeling the pragmatic intent behind asking a question. Instead of a direct translation, it first infers a plausible dialogue context and speaker goal (e.g., seeking clarification, confirming a hypothesis, or probing for missing information). The system then produces a question that is not only syntactically correct but also contextually appropriate and effective for achieving that inferred communicative goal, ensuring the questions are useful for interactive dialogue or assessment.

ideas.title: Immunological Memory Adaptation for Case Memory
ideas.description: This research adapts the immunological concept of adaptive memory refinement to knowledge-based question answering systems. In immunology, successful pathogen clearance leads to improved immunological memory through affinity maturation and clonal selection. Similarly, we propose treating each successful QA session as a learning event that triggers automatic refinement of the case memory. The system would implement mechanisms to: 1) strengthen connections between successful query patterns and answer pathways (affinity maturation), 2) expand the memory with variations of successful cases (clonal expansion), and 3) prune or reorganize ineffective memory elements based on failed sessions. This creates a dual-loop system where KBQA not only retrieves from but continuously improves the case memory through operational feedback, mimicking how biological immune systems evolve more effective responses through experience.

ideas.title: Self-knowledge for composite, multi-step reasoning

ideas.title: Structured, Entropy-Reducing Dialogue for Efficient Case Refinement
ideas.description: This system employs a dialogue manager that actively models the uncertainty within a set of initially retrieved cases. Instead of open-ended conversation, it generates minimal, structured multi-choice prompts targeting the specific features that contribute most to retrieval ambiguity. Each user selection directly updates a structured query state, and the dialogue loop terminates once a pre-defined certainty threshold is met or a maximum number of turns is reached, guaranteeing efficiency and convergence.

ideas.title: Automated Acquisition and Dynamic Quality Management for Noisy Case Memories
ideas.description: This research develops methods to automatically acquire cases from diverse, real-world sources (e.g., logs, documents, web data) without relying on full human labeling for cleaning. Crucially, it embeds a dynamic quality assessment layer that does not assume eventual perfection, but instead explicitly models the uncertainty, conflicts, and potential errors within the growing memory. The system learns to weigh, reconcile, or even leverage conflicting cases based on learned confidence scores and provenance, making the memory robustly useful for downstream tasks despite its noisy origins. This creates a closed-loop where acquisition strategies can be adjusted based on the perceived quality and utility of previously ingested data.

ideas.title: Neural Program Synthesis via Direct Semantic Grounding
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that executable programs require generation through pre-defined logical form schemas as intermediate representations. Drawing an analogy from computational linguistics, where end-to-end neural machine translation successfully bypassed traditional pipelines requiring explicit syntactic parsing and intermediate phrase-structure trees, we adapt this approach to program synthesis. The adapted idea involves training an encoder-decoder model with attention mechanisms to directly map natural language queries to executable code, learning semantic correspondences between language phrases and code structures through execution feedback, thereby eliminating the need for pre-specified logical form schemas as intermediate constraints.

ideas.title: Adaptive Grammar Induction for Logical Form Generation via Analogical Program Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts techniques from analogical modeling in computational linguistics and grammar induction to the problem of generating logical forms from retrieved examples. In the analogous field, systems learn formal grammars by identifying structural patterns and transformations between example sentences (e.g., deriving passive voice rules from active/passive pairs). The adapted solution applies this to program synthesis: retrieved logical forms are treated as example 'programs' in a formal language. The system performs analogical reasoning across these examples to induce a set of domain-specific synthesis rules or a meta-grammar. This induced model then guides the generation of novel, correct logical forms for new queries by applying learned analogical transformations, enabling more robust and generalizable synthesis than retrieval-and-patch or static template-based methods.

ideas.title: Conservation-Grounded Neural-Symbolic Integration with an Immutable Case Base
ideas.description: This research designs a hybrid system where a neural network learns to generate queries and interpret retrieved symbolic cases, but cannot directly alter the core case memory. Instead, gradients flow through a differentiable retrieval proxy to optimize how cases are selected and combined. A conservation mechanism ensures new knowledge is either accurately mapped to existing symbolic structures or, if novel and validated, added as a new immutable case. This approach achieves end-to-end learning while perfectly preserving the integrity, stability, and interpretability of the nonparametric store.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Foraging Heuristics for Computation-Efficient Few-Shot Example Selection
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Behavioral Ecology, specifically Optimal Foraging Theory, to the problem of computation-efficient few-shot selection. In nature, animals must find food efficiently under tight energy budgets, using simple, robust heuristics (like the marginal value theorem) to decide where and how long to forage. We propose to translate these principles into the machine learning context by designing selection algorithms that treat candidate training examples as 'resource patches.' The adapted solution involves creating heuristic rules based on pre-computed, static features (e.g., embedding norms, simple cluster affiliations) to quickly estimate the marginal informational gain of an example and a computational 'giving-up time' to stop searching. This approach aims to select high-quality few-shot supports with minimal online computation, bypassing expensive optimization, much like a forager efficiently maximizes energy intake without a detailed map of the entire environment.

ideas.title: Apply Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction Methods for Case Fact Pattern Embedding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology—specifically, protein-protein interaction prediction using deep metric learning—to improve semantic case retrieval. In bioinformatics, siamese or triplet networks are used to learn an embedding space where proteins with potential for interaction are proximate. We adapt this by training a similar model on legal case documents, using triplets of anchor cases, positive cases (semantically/legally similar), and negative cases (dissimilar). The model learns to generate dense vector representations where the distance between embeddings reflects legal relevance, thereby applying the dense retrieval paradigm from open-domain QA with a structured, relationship-focused training objective inspired by biological systems.

ideas.title: Verifiable Web-Scale Case Memory for Robust KBQA
ideas.description: This research investigates a method for building a massive, web-sourced case memory for KBQA that is robust to noise and misinformation. The system constructs an initial memory from web corpora but subjects all candidate cases to a rigorous cross-verification process against trusted knowledge sources and internal consistency checks. Each retained case is annotated with a confidence score and provenance, allowing the QA system to reason over uncertainty and prioritize reliable information. This approach aims to scale the benefits of case-based reasoning while directly mitigating the fatal flaw of uncurated web-scale data.

ideas.title: Bridging the Representational Gap for Multi-Modal KBQA with Neuro-Symbolic Alignment
ideas.description: This system addresses the core failure of mapping continuous multi-modal signals to discrete KB symbols by learning a unified, aligned embedding space for image regions, text tokens, and KB entities/concepts. It employs a cross-modal attention module to interpret queries, dynamically fusing visual and textual cues to form a soft, probabilistic grounding in the knowledge base. A neuro-symbolic reasoning layer then executes the query over the KB using these aligned representations, enabling robust handling of ambiguity and partial information. This approach maintains the interpretability of symbolic reasoning while overcoming the fatal representational mismatch of the original idea.

ideas.title: Improve case retrieval efficiency for large memories

ideas.title: Hybrid Legal Retrieval with Domain-Adaptive Dense Encoders for Case Search
ideas.description: This system combines a sparse lexical retriever with a dense neural retriever in a hybrid architecture to balance recall of exact legal terms with semantic matching. The core innovation is the domain-adaptive fine-tuning of the dense encoder using contrastive learning on legal corpora, including case law, to teach it legal concepts, reasoning, and factual nuance. The retrievers' results are merged via a learned ranker, optimizing for legal relevance. This approach overcomes the domain mismatch of off-the-shelf dense models, ensuring robust retrieval of both textually and semantically related precedent.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Query Fusion via Cross-Modal Hashing and Knowledge Graph Embedding
ideas.description: This idea extends CBR-KBQA to answer questions combining natural language with other modalities (like images) by drawing an analogy from bioinformatics, specifically multi-omics data integration. The analogous solution projects diverse data types into a shared latent space for unified analysis. Adapted to this context, we propose a system that uses cross-modal hashing and knowledge graph embedding techniques to project image features, text queries, and knowledge base entities into a common semantic space. This creates a unified representation where a multi-modal query can be treated holistically, enabling the retrieval and compositional reasoning of relevant KB facts regardless of the input modality mix, thereby scaling the system's capability to handle dual-modality queries effectively.

ideas.title: Automated Case Curation via Self-Supervised Quality Estimation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from astrophysical data processing to automate case acquisition and quality assessment for a memory system. In large sky surveys, automated pipelines extract, calibrate, and validate astronomical sources using intrinsic data properties and statistical flags to filter artifacts without full human labeling. Adapted to this context, the method involves creating a self-supervised curation pipeline that ingests raw case data, extracts features, and computes automated quality scores based on metrics like coherence, novelty, and informational density. Cases meeting thresholds for completeness and utility are automatically integrated into the memory, enabling scalable, high-quality case acquisition with minimal human intervention.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Stage Protein Folding to Multi-Hop CBR-KBQA
ideas.description: This idea extends CBR-KBQA to multi-hop reasoning by drawing an analogy from hierarchical protein folding in computational biology. The analogous solution uses multi-stage prediction, where stable local structures guide the assembly of the global fold. Adapted to CBR-KBQA, the system performs case-based reasoning in stages: first retrieving or generating cases for individual reasoning hops (e.g., single KB relations), using these as verified intermediate results. A subsequent CBR cycle then uses these intermediate cases as building blocks to reason towards and validate the complete multi-hop answer chain, making the complex reasoning process more tractable and traceable.

ideas.title: Structure-Aware Program Synthesis via Graph Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel method for generating executable queries or programs directly from complex, structured case representations, such as subgraphs of legal or technical documents. Instead of relying on a pre-defined logical form schema as an intermediate step, the system uses Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to encode the rich structural and relational information of a case into a latent representation. This graph-derived representation then serves as the conditioning input for a neural program synthesis model, which learns to generate syntactically and semantically correct code that operationalizes the query or reasoning implicit in the case structure. The synergy creates an end-to-end system that reasons over raw structural data to produce executable artifacts, combining the representational power of GNNs with the flexibility of schema-free program generation.

ideas.title: Dual-Safe: Verified, Multi-Stage Knowledge Curation for Robust Case Memory Maintenance
ideas.description: This system uses successful QA sessions not for direct memory modification, but to generate prioritized, confidence-scored update proposals. These proposals enter a curation pipeline where they are first checked for internal consistency with the existing knowledge graph. High-confidence, non-conflicting updates are then verified against a curated set of trusted external sources or a separate, stable reference KB. For updates that are high-impact or involve unresolved conflicts, the system flags them for lightweight human review, creating a safeguard against error propagation. This ensures the case memory is expanded and corrected reliably, maintaining long-term system integrity.

ideas.title: Retrieval by Structural Complementarity via Molecular Docking Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that retrieval must rely on surface similarity between query and document. Drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically molecular docking in drug discovery, the core problem is parallel: finding a match based on functional complementarity rather than resemblance. The analogous solution involves simulating the fit between a target protein's active site and candidate ligands based on 3D shape and chemical property complementarity. Adapted to retrieval, the research would develop methods to model a query's latent compositional structure (its 'binding site')—such as its missing premises, required logical operators, or unresolved relational slots—and then retrieve text passages that best complement this structure. Success would be measured by how well the retrieved text functionally completes or satisfies the underlying compositional need of the query, moving beyond lexical or semantic similarity to a form of structural and functional retrieval.

ideas.title: Robust Case-Based Reasoning with Temporally Noisy Memory and Evolving Ontologies
ideas.description: This research direction investigates methods for case-based reasoning systems that must operate when two critical assumptions are simultaneously relaxed: the case memory contains noisy, conflicting, or outdated facts, and the knowledge schema (ontology, relations, entity types) itself evolves after cases are stored. The synergy creates a core challenge of disentangling whether a retrieved case is discordant due to original observational error, a subsequent shift in schema meaning, or both. The novel capability is the development of temporal robustness metrics and repair strategies that can revise or annotate stored cases with confidence scores and schema-version tags, allowing for principled reasoning across a corpus of imperfect memories recorded under different conceptual regimes.

ideas.title: Distrust-and-Verify Framework for Robust Autonomous Case Acquisition
ideas.description: This system automates case acquisition while prioritizing robustness over volume by assuming all incoming data is potentially erroneous or adversarial. It uses a cascading validation pipeline with redundant checks, including cross-validation against known high-quality cases, consistency testing via logical constraints, and adversarial filtering using generated counterexamples. A key innovation is a meta-monitor that tracks the validator's own performance, triggering calibrated human oversight or halting ingestion when it detects distribution shifts or emerging failure modes, ensuring the memory's integrity is never fully delegated to the automated process.

ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple robustness to noisy memory by implementing an active, meta-cognitive layer that evaluates the trustworthiness of each case in real-time. It computes a dynamic reliability score by analyzing internal consistency, agreement with a peer-set of cases, and alignment with background knowledge constraints. This allows the system to automatically curate its case memory, correcting or isolating errors while intelligently preserving rare but correct cases that are crucial for handling edge scenarios and avoiding catastrophic forgetting of valid knowledge.

ideas.title: Adopt Molecular Fingerprinting GNNs for Case Representation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry. In drug discovery, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are successfully used to generate 'molecular fingerprints'—vector embeddings that encode the complex structural and functional properties of a molecule graph for similarity search. We adapt this solution to legal case retrieval. A legal case is represented as a structured subgraph (e.g., with nodes for legal concepts, parties, and rulings, and edges for their relationships). A GNN is then trained to produce a fixed-dimensional 'case fingerprint' embedding that holistically captures the case's legal reasoning structure. This approach allows for retrieval based on deep structural and relational similarities between cases, moving beyond traditional text-based methods to better identify analogous legal principles and outcomes.

ideas.title: Verified Program Synthesis with Neurosymbolic Fallback for Robust Logical Form Generation
ideas.description: This system combines inductive program synthesis with a verification and fallback mechanism to ensure robustness. The synthesis engine proposes candidate logical forms from retrieved examples, which are then rigorously checked for consistency and correctness using a symbolic verifier. If verification fails, a fallback neural generator, trained to respect logical constraints, produces the final output. This hybrid design ensures high precision from synthesis while maintaining coverage and reliability on out-of-distribution queries.

ideas.title: Adapting Model Selection Criteria from Astrophysics's Exoplanet Detection Validation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysics, specifically the statistical validation of exoplanet detections without direct follow-up observation. In that field, astronomers validate a candidate by using the single observed light curve to calculate a False Positive Probability (FPP), combining multiple diagnostic lines of evidence to rule out alternative scenarios like binary stars. Adapted to true few-shot model selection, the research would develop a new criterion that avoids held-out data. It would work by treating a candidate model/prompt's performance on the support set as a 'signal' to be validated. The method would compute a 'False Performance Degradation Probability' (FPDP) by analyzing several internal diagnostics from that single set—such as prediction consistency across prompt variations, entropy of in-context predictions, and sensitivity to example ordering—to synthetically model and rule out overfitting or instability scenarios. The selection criterion would then be to choose the configuration that minimizes this composite FPDP, providing a robust, data-efficient alternative to cross-validation or MDL.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Locality-Sensitive Hashing for Memory Retrieval
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from computational biology for querying massive genomic sequence databases to the problem of efficient case retrieval in large nonparametric memories. In genomics, hierarchical indexing structures like multi-level Locality-Sensitive Hashing and metric trees are used to avoid comparing a query against every sequence in the database. The adapted solution involves developing a hierarchical, multi-tiered index for the case memory. Cases are organized using progressively refined similarity hashes or sketches, enabling retrieval to quickly prune irrelevant branches of the memory and focus computational resources on only the most promising subsets of cases, thereby achieving sub-linear search times as the memory scales.

ideas.title: Semantically-Grounded Program Synthesis via Emergent Logical Scaffolding
ideas.description: This system generates executable queries without a fixed logical form schema by co-constructing a minimal, verifiable logical scaffold during the generation process. It uses a neuro-symbolic component to dynamically infer type constraints and predicate-argument relationships from the interaction context and the target knowledge base or API. This ensures each step of the generated program is semantically grounded and consistent before proceeding, preventing nonsensical outputs. The result is robust, executable code that is both flexible in form and faithful in meaning.

ideas.title: Robust Case-Based Reasoning via Error-Correcting Code Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a clean, correct case memory by developing robustness to erroneous or conflicting cases, analogous to how digital communications handle noisy transmissions. In Information Theory, error-correcting codes add structured redundancy to data, enabling the detection and correction of errors. Adapted to case-based reasoning, we propose encoding cases with internal redundancy—such as correlated features, derived abstractions, or meta-constraints—creating a 'case code.' During retrieval, the system can use this redundancy to detect inconsistencies, resolve conflicts between similar but differing cases, or reconstruct plausible missing elements, thereby 'correcting' noisy or imperfect memories before they are applied to new problems. This approach shifts robustness from perfect storage to intelligent, redundancy-driven reconstruction.

ideas.title: Temporally-Vetted Knowledge Base Evolution with Version-Aware Reasoning
ideas.description: This system explicitly models knowledge base schema evolution as a sequence of temporally-scoped, validated versions. Proposed changes to entities or relations must pass through a vetting layer that assesses their provenance, consistency with existing facts, and corroboration from multiple sources before being committed. The system maintains a full history of all schema states, enabling queries to be resolved with respect to a specific point in time or across versions, ensuring robust reasoning even as the underlying knowledge representation changes. This approach guarantees both adaptability and auditability, preventing corruption from erroneous updates.
